title: Futility
description: Futility — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "Futility" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata

memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Table of Contents'

memes.label: Admiral Butt
memes.description: Admiral Butt, the British naval officer sent to Russia before the revolution, whose combination of military authority and comic domesticity makes him a memorable figure in William Gerhardie's Futility.

memes.label: Allied Forces in Siberia
memes.description: The multinational Allied military forces deployed to Siberia during the Russian Civil War, whose British contingent provides several key characters in William Gerhardie's Futility.

memes.label: Allied Intervention in Siberia
memes.description: The Allied military intervention in Siberia (1918–1922), depicted in Gerhardie's Futility as a "comic opera" of futility — a historical episode experienced through personal chaos and bureaucratic farce.

memes.label: Andrei Andreiech
memes.description: The first-person narrator of Gerhardie's Futility — a young Anglo-Russian, graduate of Oxford, caught between cultures and in love with Nina Bursanov, observing the chaos around him with ironic detachment that masks genuine feeling.

memes.label: Baron Wunderhausen
memes.description: Baron Wunderhausen, a German-Russian baron who marries Sonia and serves as both comic relief and unlikely philosophical voice in William Gerhardie's Futility.

memes.label: Beauty in Futility
memes.description: The place of beauty and aesthetic experience in William Gerhardie's novel Futility.

memes.label: Bolsheviks
memes.description: The revolutionary faction whose rise to power in 1917 forms the backdrop of Futility — simultaneously a political reality, a military adversary, and a rhetorical catch-all for everything the novel's displaced characters have lost.

memes.label: Bolshevism in Futility
memes.description: International Bolshevism as a political force and narrative backdrop in Gerhardie's Futility — treated with comic irony rather than ideological seriousness.

memes.label: British Embassy in Petrograd
memes.description: The British diplomatic mission in Petrograd, where the narrator Andrei Andreiech works and the Admiral holds court — a pocket of English order amid Russian chaos in Gerhardie's Futility.

memes.label: Captain Larkin
memes.description: Captain Larkin is a minor character in William Gerhardie's novel Futility, a US Navy officer who appears briefly in a dinner-table debate about military discipline.

memes.label: Cecedek
memes.description: Cecedek, the Austrian whom Fanny Ivanovna was expected to marry, a figure of financial entanglement and romantic evasion in William Gerhardie's Futility.

memes.label: Characters by Nationality
memes.description: Futility characters and historical figures grouped by nationality, reflecting the novel's international milieu during the Russian Civil War.

memes.label: Chekhov in Futility
memes.description: The influence of Anton Chekhov on William Gerhardie's novel Futility, particularly through the reference to Three Sisters.

memes.label: Class and Society in Futility
memes.description: Social class in Gerhardie's Futility — the decay of the Russian aristocracy, the precarious status of the displaced, and the comic incongruity of old hierarchies in a revolutionary world.

memes.label: Comedy and Tragedy in Futility
memes.description: The Chekhovian blurring of comedy and tragedy as the central aesthetic mode of Gerhardie's Futility — a "No Man's Land" between laughter and despair.

memes.label: Concept Index
memes.description: Complete index of all concept pages in the Futility wiki, sorted alphabetically.

memes.label: Czech Legion
memes.description: The Czechoslovak Legion, an Allied-aligned military force that controlled the Trans-Siberian Railway during the Russian Civil War, appearing in Futility as both strategic factor and object of the characters' frustrated commentary.

memes.label: Death in Futility
memes.description: The treatment of death — as personal loss, looming threat, and philosophical question — in William Gerhardie's novel Futility.

memes.label: "Dedication"
memes.description: "'Futility' — dedication to Katherine Mansfield."

memes.label: Disillusionment in Futility
memes.description: Disillusionment as the governing emotional condition of characters in William Gerhardie's Futility, where the loss of illusions is at once a source of wisdom and a guarantee of misery.

memes.label: Dreams in Futility
memes.description: The motif of dreams and dreaming as a mode of escape, premonition, and emotional truth in William Gerhardie's novel Futility.

memes.label: Duty in Futility
memes.description: The contested role of duty — military, familial, and moral obligation — as a force that both motivates and entraps characters in William Gerhardie's novel Futility.

memes.label: Eberheim
memes.description: Eberheim, an elderly German gentleman married into the family circle for financial reasons, whose illness and groans provide darkly comic relief in William Gerhardie's Futility.

memes.label: Eisenstein
memes.description: A Jewish dentist who ran away with Nikolai Vasilievich's wife, lived off her money, and followed the family across Russia during the Revolution.

memes.label: England
memes.description: England as the narrator's homeland and the source of British official presence in revolutionary Russia, a distant point of reference in William Gerhardie's Futility.

memes.label: Event Index
memes.description: Complete index of all event pages in the Futility wiki, sorted alphabetically.

memes.label: Family in Futility
memes.description: The Bursanov family as the dysfunctional domestic centre of Gerhardie's Futility — an "Ibsen drama" of retrospective revelations, financial dependence, and impossible attachments.

memes.label: Fanny Ivanovna
memes.description: The matriarch of the Bursanov family in Gerhardie's Futility — German-born, endlessly resourceful, given to self-dramatization, and the novel's most vivid and voluble presence.

memes.label: Fate in Futility
memes.description: The treatment of fate as an inescapable yet negotiable force in William Gerhardie's novel Futility, where characters variously resist, submit to, or attempt to bribe their destinies.

memes.label: Faust (Gounod)
memes.description: Charles Gounod's opera Faust, based on Goethe's tragedy, which is referenced in William Gerhardie's Futility as part of the novel's musical and literary texture.

memes.label: Fear in Futility
memes.description: The role of fear — as personal anxiety, political dread, and existential unease — in William Gerhardie's novel Futility.

memes.label: February Revolution (1917)
memes.description: The February Revolution of 1917, which overthrew the Tsarist regime and established the Provisional Government, as the political rupture that transforms the world of William Gerhardie's Futility.

memes.label: Freedom in Futility
memes.description: The theme of freedom and its elusiveness in William Gerhardie's novel Futility.

memes.label: "Futility"
memes.description: "'Futility' — a novel by William Gerhardie, first published in 1922."

memes.label: Futility (Theme)
memes.description: The central theme of William Gerhardie's novel — the sense that human endeavour, especially in love and politics, is ultimately futile, yet this very futility becomes a source of absurd vitality.

memes.label: General Bologoevski
memes.description: General Bologoevski, a silver-haired Russian general of British sympathies whose endless monologues, explosive curses, and social ubiquity make him one of the great comic figures in William Gerhardie's Futility.

memes.label: General Wrangel
memes.description: General Pyotr Wrangel was a leading White Army commander during the Russian Civil War, whose army's disbandment in Constantinople is mentioned in William Gerhardie's Futility.

memes.label: Germany
memes.description: Germany as the homeland of Fanny Ivanovna and other German-Russian characters, a source of identity, suspicion, and displacement in William Gerhardie's Futility.

memes.label: Happiness in Futility
memes.description: The paradoxical treatment of happiness in Gerhardie's Futility — a state that blinds, that is always precarious, and that feels most acutely when already lost.

memes.label: Home in Futility
memes.description: The motif of home and homelessness in William Gerhardie's novel Futility, set against the backdrop of post-revolutionary Russian exile.

memes.label: Hope in Futility
memes.description: The paradoxical persistence of hope amid the pervasive hopelessness that defines the emotional landscape of William Gerhardie's Futility.

memes.label: Ibsen's Influence on Futility
memes.description: The influence of Henrik Ibsen's dramatic method on William Gerhardie's Futility, explicitly acknowledged in the novel's opening sentence.

memes.label: Identity in Futility
memes.description: The exploration of personal and national identity in William Gerhardie's novel Futility.

memes.label: Illusion in Futility
memes.description: The role of illusion and self-deception as psychological survival mechanisms in William Gerhardie's novel Futility.

memes.label: Japan
memes.description: Japan as a presence on the eastern edge of the novel's geography, associated with the Allied Intervention in Siberia and the Far Eastern dimension of the Russian Civil War in William Gerhardie's Futility.

memes.label: Kniaz
memes.description: Kniaz, an elderly fixture of the family circle whose name means "Prince" and who serves as Nikolai Vasilievich's partner in the Siberian goldmine scheme in William Gerhardie's Futility.

memes.label: Kolchak's Coup
memes.description: Admiral Kolchak's coup of November 1918, which established him as Supreme Ruler of Russia and whose aftermath forms the immediate political context of the Siberian sections of William Gerhardie's Futility.

memes.label: Kolchak's Army
memes.description: The anti-Bolshevik military regime of Admiral Alexander Kolchak, established at Omsk in 1918, whose doomed resistance against the rising tide of [[russia|Russian]] revolutionary change forms the political backdrop of the later chapters of Futility.

memes.label: Lies in Futility
memes.description: The motif of lies and self-deception as a central thematic concern in William Gerhardie's novel Futility.

memes.label: Love in Futility
memes.description: Romantic love in Gerhardie's Futility — obsessive, unrequited, and inextricable from the chaos of revolution and family dysfunction.

memes.label: Madame Olenin
memes.description: Madame Olenin is a minor character in William Gerhardie's novel Futility, a relative of Uncle Kostia and mother of Olya and the infant Fanny.

memes.label: Magda Nikolaevna
memes.description: Magda Nikolaevna is the mother of the three sisters—Sonia, Nina, and Vera—in William Gerhardie's novel Futility, a delicate beauty who travels with the family through the chaos of revolution and civil war.

memes.label: Marriage in Futility
memes.description: The role of marriage as a failed social institution in William Gerhardie's Futility, where formal bonds are repeatedly undermined by infidelity, financial dependency, and the characters' yearning for conventional respectability.

memes.label: Melancholy in Futility
memes.description: Melancholy as the dominant emotional register and atmospheric quality of William Gerhardie's novel Futility, linked to the Russian landscape and national character.

memes.label: Memory in Futility
memes.description: The function of memory and the past as a structural and thematic element in William Gerhardie's novel Futility.

memes.label: Moesei Moeseiech
memes.description: Moesei Moeseiech is a minor character in William Gerhardie's novel Futility, a tearful hanger-on of the Nikolai Vasilievich family whose emotional vulnerability contrasts with the cool detachment of the three sisters.

memes.label: Mohovaya Street
memes.description: Mohovaya Street is a real street in Petrograd (St. Petersburg) that appears as a location in Futility.

memes.label: Money in Futility
memes.description: The role of money — its loss, its irregular flow, its power to bind and separate — as a structural principle in Gerhardie's Futility.

memes.label: Moscow
memes.description: Moscow as the family's original home and a symbol of pre-revolutionary Russian life, frequently referenced as the place characters have left behind in William Gerhardie's Futility.

memes.label: Motherhood in Futility
memes.description: The role of motherhood as a source of both moral authority and profound ambivalence in William Gerhardie's Futility, where maternal identity is a contested terrain between duty, love, and self-preservation.

memes.label: Mr Thomson
memes.description: Mr Thomson is Nikolai Vasilievich's British consulting engineer in William Gerhardie's novel Futility, who returns to England during the Russian Civil War.

memes.label: Music in Futility
memes.description: The role of music — piano playing, singing, opera, and musical comedy — as a cultural and emotional register in Gerhardie's Futility.

memes.label: Nature in Futility
memes.description: The role of nature and landscape — particularly the Siberian wilderness — as both setting and symbolic force in William Gerhardie's novel Futility.

memes.label: Nikolai Vasilievich
memes.description: The patriarch of the Bursanov family in Gerhardie's Futility — obstinate, financially indispensable, emotionally opaque, and the gravitational centre around which the family's chaos orbits.

memes.label: Nina
memes.description: The middle Bursanov sister in Gerhardie's Futility — the narrator's elusive love interest, possessing steel-grey eyes, a disquieting sidelong look, and her father's obstinacy.

memes.label: Omsk
memes.description: Omsk, the Siberian city that served as Admiral Kolchak's capital during the Russian Civil War and features as a key setting in the later sections of William Gerhardie's Futility.

memes.label: Organization Index
memes.description: Complete index of all organization pages in the Futility wiki, sorted alphabetically.

memes.label: Oscar Wilde in Futility
memes.description: The presence of Oscar Wilde — his works, his wit, and his aesthetic stance — as a cultural reference point in William Gerhardie's Futility, most notably through Zina's quotation.

memes.label: Oxford
memes.description: Oxford in Futility — a symbol of English stability that the narrator leaves, returns to, and finds diminished; the counterpoint to Petrograd and Vladivostok in the novel's geography of displacement.

memes.label: Palace Square
memes.description: Palace Square in Petrograd is a landmark in William Gerhardie's Futility, where the narrator walks through it on the night of the February Revolution, the square's imperial grandeur contrasting with the chaos of revolutionary upheaval.

memes.label: Passion in Futility
memes.description: The role of passion — romantic, political, and aesthetic — as a driving force and source of absurdity in William Gerhardie's novel Futility.

memes.label: Person Index
memes.description: Complete index of all person pages in the Futility wiki, sorted alphabetically.

memes.label: Petrograd
memes.description: The imperial capital and primary setting of Gerhardie's Futility — a city of embassies, theatres, and the Mohovaya flat, poised between its aristocratic past and revolutionary future.

memes.label: Place Index
memes.description: Complete index of all place pages in the Futility wiki, sorted alphabetically.

memes.label: "Preface"
memes.description: "'Futility' — author's preface by William Gerhardie."

memes.label: Pride in Futility
memes.description: The role of pride as a motivating and complicating force in William Gerhardie's novel Futility.

memes.label: Professor Metchnikoff
memes.description: Professor Metchnikoff is a horse in William Gerhardie's novel Futility, named with ironic pomposity and serving as a recurring comic motif.

memes.label: Russian Provisional Government
memes.description: The Russian Provisional Government, the short-lived authority that ruled Russia between the February and October Revolutions of 1917, whose weakness and fall are part of the political trajectory of William Gerhardie's Futility.

memes.label: Reason in Futility
memes.description: The tension between reason and irrationality as a recurring thematic concern in William Gerhardie's novel Futility.

memes.label: Red Army
memes.description: The Red Army, the Bolshevik military force created by Leon Trotsky that defeated the White Army in the Russian Civil War, as the emerging power in the background of William Gerhardie's Futility.

memes.label: Religion in Futility
memes.description: The treatment of religion and religious faith in William Gerhardie's novel Futility.

memes.label: Russia
memes.description: The sprawling backdrop of Gerhardie's Futility — a nation in the throes of revolution, civil war, and foreign intervention, perceived through the fractured lens of its bewildered inhabitants and expatriate observers.

memes.label: Russian Civil War
memes.description: The multi-sided armed conflict (1917–1922) between Bolshevik Red forces and anti-Bolshevik White armies, forming the military and political backdrop of Futility's later chapters.

memes.label: Russian Revolution in Futility
memes.description: The 1917 Russian Revolution as experienced and depicted in Gerhardie's Futility — a world-historical event encountered through comic opera chaos, bureaucratic absurdity, and personal entanglement.

memes.label: Russian Revolution (1917)
memes.description: The February and October Revolutions of 1917 that overthrew the Tsarist regime and brought the Bolsheviks to power, forming the political backdrop of Futility.

memes.label: Russian Soul in Futility
memes.description: The concept of the "Russian soul" as explored in William Gerhardie's Futility — a national character at once grand, incoherent, and unknowable, filtered through the dual Anglo-Russian perspective of the narrator.

memes.label: Sacrifice in Futility
memes.description: The theme of sacrifice — its nobility, futility, and frequent status as self-deception — in William Gerhardie's novel Futility.

memes.label: Scriabin in Futility
memes.description: References to the Russian composer Alexander Scriabin in William Gerhardie's novel Futility.

memes.label: Shame in Futility
memes.description: The role of shame as a social and psychological force in William Gerhardie's novel Futility.

memes.label: Shanghai
memes.description: Shanghai appears in Futility as one of the Far Eastern cities that formed part of the Russian exile route after the Revolution.

memes.label: Siberia
memes.description: The vast Russian territory traversed by the Trans-Siberian Railway, serving as the primary setting for the Allied Intervention chapters and the family's eastward displacement in Futility.

memes.label: Sir Hugo
memes.description: Sir Hugo, a British official in Siberia whose catchphrase "Splendid!" and imperturbable politeness make him one of the most distinctive comic figures in William Gerhardie's Futility.

memes.label: Sonia
memes.description: Eldest daughter of the Nikolai Vasilievich family, married to Baron Wunderhausen, embodying the futility of strategic marriage in William Gerhardie's Futility.

memes.label: Stanitski
memes.description: Stanitski, Nikolai Vasilievich's bookkeeper, a minor but recurring character whose emotional volatility and optimistic disposition provide comic relief in William Gerhardie's Futility.

memes.label: Suffering in Futility
memes.description: The portrayal of suffering as a transformative yet ultimately futile experience in William Gerhardie's novel Futility.

memes.label: Themes in Futility
memes.description: A thematic index of all concept pages exploring the major themes of William Gerhardie's Futility — love, war, futility itself, and the emotional landscape of exile.

memes.label: Three Sisters (Chekhov)
memes.description: Anton Chekhov's 1901 play Three Sisters — the theatrical model and explicit reference point for Gerhardie's Futility, within which the Bursanov sisters recognize their own tragedy.

memes.label: Time in Futility
memes.description: The treatment of time — its passage, its suspension, and its role in creating the novel's atmosphere of waiting and futility — in William Gerhardie's novel Futility.

memes.label: Treaty of Brest-Litovsk
memes.description: The Treaty of Brest-Litovsk (March 1918), which ended Russia's participation in World War I and whose consequences ripple through the political landscape of William Gerhardie's Futility.

memes.label: Tristan und Isolde (Wagner)
memes.description: Richard Wagner's opera Tristan und Isolde, referenced in William Gerhardie's novel Futility as a cultural touchstone.

memes.label: Truth in Futility
memes.description: The treatment of truth and half-truth as thematic concerns in William Gerhardie's novel Futility.

memes.label: Uncle Kostia
memes.description: The philosopher-writer of the Bursanov household in Gerhardie's Futility — the character who most explicitly articulates the novel's theme of futility, discovering in physical uselessness a sense of spiritual importance.

memes.label: Vera
memes.description: Youngest of the three sisters in Nikolai Vasilievich's family, the most rebellious and emotionally volatile, in William Gerhardie's Futility.

memes.label: Vladivostok
memes.description: Far Eastern port city where the Nikolai Vasilievich family and the narrator take refuge during the Russian Civil War, the terminal point of Futility's geography of displacement.

memes.label: War in Futility
memes.description: The thematic treatment of war — particularly World War I and the Russian Civil War — as a backdrop and disruptor of personal lives in William Gerhardie's novel Futility.

memes.label: White Army
memes.description: The White Army, the anti-Bolshevik military forces who fought the Reds in the Russian Civil War and whose cause provides the political backdrop of William Gerhardie's Futility.

memes.label: Work Index
memes.description: Complete index of all work pages in the Futility wiki, sorted alphabetically.

memes.label: World War I
memes.description: World War I (1914–1918) as the historical backdrop against which the events of William Gerhardie's Futility unfold, shaping the fates of all its characters.

memes.label: Zina
memes.description: Zina (Zinaida), the young Russian woman whom Nikolai Vasilievich is expected to marry, representing familial obligation and the complications of romantic entanglement in Futility.

memes.label: Zolotoy Rog
memes.description: Zolotoy Rog (the "Golden Horn") is a famous restaurant in Vladivostok, nicknamed the "Solitary Dog" by British sailors, where characters in William Gerhardie's Futility dine and socialize during the Siberian intervention.
